text: Oxygen cost of running (ml·kg⁻¹·km⁻¹) does not significantly change across submaximal speeds ranging from 75% to 95% of lactate threshold speed in trained male distance runners.
Caloric unit cost (kcal·kg⁻¹·km⁻¹) significantly increases with running speed across submaximal intensities, even when oxygen cost per kilometer remains constant.
specific_value: 1.05 to 1.11 kcal·kg⁻¹·km⁻¹ from 75% to 95% LT speed, P < 0.001

text: Expressing running economy as caloric unit cost (kcal·kg⁻¹·km⁻¹) is more sensitive to speed-related changes than oxygen cost (ml·kg⁻¹·km⁻¹) because it accounts for differences in substrate utilization at different intensities.
Substrate utilization shifts at higher submaximal running speeds contribute to increased energy cost per kilometer even when oxygen cost per kilometer remains unchanged.
Typical oxygen cost of running at submaximal speeds on a flat treadmill is approximately 217-221 ml·kg⁻¹·km⁻¹ in trained male distance runners.
